title: Beer on Carpet
timeEstimate: 15–30 minutes; one of the easier tannin stains
stainChemistry: Beer contains light malt tannins (lower concentration than wine, coffee, or tea), hop resins, barley protein, and sugar (maltose / fermentable residuals). On carpet, fresh beer is light-colored and mostly water-soluble — it's one of the EASIER tannin stains. Dried beer darkens as tannins oxidize and sugar caramelizes, creating a sticky tan halo that attracts dirt for weeks if not flushed early. Dark beer (stout / porter) carries more melanoidin pigment from the roasted malt and stains harder than light beer. Same tannin rule as all the tannin family: NO alkaline cleaners (ammonia, baking soda, OxiClean) — they turn tannin darker permanently. Cold water + Dawn + peroxide + vinegar sequence clears most cases in one round.
whyThisWorks: Beer's low tannin concentration means fresh beer lifts out with Dawn + cold water + 3% peroxide easily. Sugar flush first (cold water) prevents sticky residue from caramelizing or attracting dirt. Weighted paper towels overnight prevent pad wick-back ring.
defaultAssumption: Party / pub context — spilled glass, dropped bottle, overfilled stein. Fresh = trivially easy. Overnight with sugar caramelized = harder, but still reasonable home win. Dark beer (stout) = budget an extra peroxide round. Spilled CASE of beer on carpet = pad saturation likely, pro.

spottingProtocol.technique: Absorb liquid before it reaches pad
spottingProtocol.instruction: Press paper towels straight down on the spill. Lift, rotate, press. Work outside-in. Don't rub. Keep blotting until towels come up dry.
Small amount of cold water on the spot, blot. Repeat 2–3 times. Removes sugar + fresh tannin. Cold only. Don't flood — pad wick.
Dab the Dawn + peroxide mix (step 4) on a hidden carpet area 2 min. Color transfer = skip peroxide, Dawn alone. Clean = proceed.

spottingProtocol.equipment: 1 tbsp Dawn + 1 cup 3% H₂O₂ on damp cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Mix Dawn + 3% peroxide. Dampen white cloth, wring, press 5–10 min. Blot. Light beer usually clears in 1 cycle. Dark beer / stout: 2 cycles.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Never mix with ammonia / bleach / alkaline.

spottingProtocol.technique: Neutralize + keep tannin on acid side
spottingProtocol.equipment: 1 tbsp vinegar + 1 cup cool water on damp cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Vinegar rinse on damp cloth 5 min. Neutralizes peroxide, keeps tannin light. Blot dry.
Weighted paper towels overnight. Fan-dry in morning. Prevents sugar + tannin wick-back. Inspect in daylight — halo = one more peroxide round.

steps: 2. Cold water sugar flush + blot between.
4. Dawn + 3% H₂O₂ mix damp cloth, 5–10 min, 1–2 cycles.
warnings: NEVER alkaline cleaners (baking soda / OxiClean / ammonia) on tannin
Hidden peroxide test on dyed carpet
Dark beer / stout: expect 2 peroxide cycles

homeSolutions.agent: Good News — Beer Is an Easier Tannin Stain
homeSolutions.instruction: Beer has much less tannin than red wine, coffee, or tea — fresh beer usually lifts out in one round. The rules that matter: (1) NO alkaline cleaners — no baking soda, OxiClean, ammonia, all-purpose sprays. They turn tannin darker permanently. (2) Don't let the sugar dry into a sticky halo — flush it with cold water fast. Your toolkit: cold water, Dawn, 3% hydrogen peroxide, white vinegar.

Press a thick stack of white paper towels straight down on the spill. Lift, move to a dry section, press again. Work from the outside of the spill inward. Don't rub — rubbing mashes beer + sugar into the pad, where it comes back as a ring.
Pour a SMALL amount of cold water on the spot, blot with a dry cloth, repeat 2–3 times. Removes sugar + fresh tannin. Cold only — hot water cross-links tannin. Don't flood — pad wick is the enemy.
In a hidden carpet area, dab the Dawn + peroxide mix on a cloth, press 2 minutes. Carpet color transfers = skip peroxide, use Dawn alone. Clean = proceed.

Mix 1 tablespoon Dawn with 1 cup 3% drugstore hydrogen peroxide. Dampen a white cloth, wring it out, press on the spot 5–10 minutes. Blot with a clean damp cloth. Light beer (lagers / pilsners) usually clears in one round. Dark beer (stout, porter, brown ale) may need a second round.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix peroxide with ammonia, bleach, OxiClean, or baking soda.

homeSolutions.instruction: Mix 1 tablespoon white vinegar + 1 cup cool water. Dab on a damp cloth, press 5 minutes. Neutralizes peroxide, keeps any residual tannin on the light (acid) side. Blot dry.

Stack dry paper towels on the spot, put a heavy book on top, leave overnight. Pulls residual beer sugar + tannin out of the pad before it wicks back as a halo. Fan-dry in the morning. Inspect in daylight. Halo remaining = repeat steps 5–6.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate for Case-Size Spills or Wool
homeSolutions.instruction: Pro if the spill is big enough to saturate the pad (someone spilled a whole six-pack), if the carpet is wool, or if alkaline cleaner was used before you got there (tannin locked). Tell them: "Beer on [nylon / polyester / wool] carpet, [light / dark], age [fresh / overnight], home-treated with cold + Dawn + 3% peroxide + vinegar. No alkaline used. [Halo remains / pad saturation / alkaline was used before]."

steps: 2. NO alkaline cleaners — no baking soda / OxiClean.
5. Dawn + 3% peroxide mix damp cloth, 5–10 min, 1–2 cycles.
8. Large spill / wool / locked tannin = pro.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if alkaline cleaner was used first (tannin locked), if pad is saturated from a large spill, or if ring returns after two rounds.
warnings: Hidden peroxide test on dyed carpet

neverDo: Never baking soda / OxiClean / ammonia on beer
Never mix peroxide with bleach / ammonia
fiberSensitivities: Nylon / polyester / olefin carpet: tolerates full protocol
Wool: hidden test, peroxide risky — pro lane for tight spots
Dyed carpet: hidden peroxide test mandatory

materialWarnings: Easier tannin than wine / coffee / tea
Dark beer takes an extra cycle

escalation.when: Case-size spill (pad saturation), wool carpet, alkaline cleaner used before treatment, or ring returns after two home rounds.
escalation.whatToTell: "Beer on [nylon / polyester / wool] carpet, [light / dark], age [fresh / overnight], home-treated with cold + Dawn + 3% peroxide + vinegar. No alkaline used [or: alkaline was applied before I started]. [Residual halo / pad saturation]."
escalation.specialistType: professional carpet cleaner with tannin chemistry

contextQuestions: Light beer (lager, pilsner) or dark (stout, porter)?
How much was spilled — glass, pitcher, case?
Has anyone applied OxiClean / baking soda?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Light beer is the easy case — usually a one-round job. Dark beer has melanoidin pigment from roasted malts and takes an extra cycle. Case-size spills (a whole six-pack) saturate the pad and become a pro extraction job.
